skill.name: canton-network-repos
skill.description: Guide to Canton Network open-source repositories, DAML SDK versions, Canton runtime builds, and Splice application compatibility.
skill.summary: Map DAML, Canton, and Splice repository relationships for version and build work.
skill.author: 0xBigBoss

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, filesystem, environment, and cryptography patterns. Review found a Markdown guide with fenced examples and reference links, not executable code or a prompt injection attempt. The residual risk is that agents may run documented shell commands or file edits in a user repository.

medium_findings.title: User-Actionable Shell and File Modification Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill documents build commands and repository edits, including sbt, nix-shell, perl in-place replacement, and DAML build commands. These are legitimate guide content, but an agent could execute them in a user workspace and modify files or consume local build resources without explicit review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are present in fenced documentation and support the skill purpose. Risk remains because they include executable build steps and bulk file edits that affect a local repository.
medium_findings.remediation: Require user confirmation before running commands or applying repository-wide replacements from this guide.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: Most external command detections are Markdown fenced examples, diagrams, tables, or inline code. They are not Ruby backtick execution and do not execute by being installed as a skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are Markdown fences or inline documentation. No executable wrapper, script file, or runtime hook was found in the scanned file.
low_findings.remediation: No code change is required. Marketplace presentation should still warn users before executing copied commands.

low_findings.title: Reference URLs Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to official release pages and documentation. No evidence found that the skill sends data to these URLs or performs network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear under an External References heading and are plain Markdown links. There is no fetch, curl, webhook, or data exfiltration logic in the file.
low_findings.remediation: Keep links visible as documentation references and avoid automatic browsing without user intent.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Mention Is Build Configuration Context
low_findings.description: The sensitive access finding is an example of Splice build configuration using DAML_COMPILER_VERSION. No evidence found that the skill reads, prints, stores, or transmits environment values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable appears in a Scala snippet explaining repository configuration. The skill file itself has no executable path for accessing environment variables.
low_findings.remediation: Do not ask agents to reveal environment values when troubleshooting builds.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal and Weak Crypto Alerts Are Terminology False Positives
low_findings.description: The path traversal alert points to an ellipsis in a reference path, and the weak cryptography alerts align with domain terms such as hashes and package IDs. No evidence found of path traversal logic or weak algorithm use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines describe package ID concepts, troubleshooting, and abbreviated file paths. They do not include file-open logic, traversal input, MD5, SHA1, or cryptographic implementation code.
low_findings.remediation: No remediation is needed beyond preserving the documentation context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented Commands May Be Executed by an Agent
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes executable shell commands and in-place file replacement examples. The pattern is acceptable for a build guide but requires explicit user consent before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are clear executable commands, but they are presented as documentation. The safety outcome depends on whether an assistant runs them automatically.
dangerous_patterns.remediation: Treat command snippets as advisory until the user approves command execution in a specific repository.

content.value_statement: Canton Network projects can be hard to align across DAML SDK, Canton runtime, and Splice releases. This skill gives repository maps, version guidance, and build context for safer compatibility work.
actual_capabilities: Explains the relationship between DAML SDK, Canton, and Splice repositories.
Maps known Splice, Canton, DAML SDK, protocol, and DAML-LF version combinations.
Identifies key files for LF versions, package targets, Canton versions, and Docker builds.
Describes build steps for community Canton participants and selected Splice packages.
Explains package ID implications when changing DAML-LF target versions.
Provides troubleshooting context for DAML-LF version and package ID mismatch errors.
limitations: It does not verify the latest upstream release state automatically.
It is based on the information embedded in the skill file.
Build commands require local tools such as sbt, nix-shell, and DAML SDK.
It does not replace official release notes or security advisories.

use_cases.title: Plan a Version Upgrade
use_cases.description: Compare DAML SDK, Canton, and Splice version expectations before changing DAML-LF targets or SDK versions.

use_cases.title: Debug Package Mismatches
use_cases.description: Trace package ID and upgrade validation failures back to LF target versions, SDK versions, and dependency differences.

use_cases.title: Navigate Repository Structure
use_cases.description: Find the repositories and files that control Canton runtime builds, Splice packages, and DAML compiler behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Find the Right Repository
prompt_templates.prompt: I am new to Canton Network. Explain which repository I should inspect for DAML SDK, Canton runtime, and Splice application changes.

prompt_templates.title: Check Version Compatibility
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare my Splice, Canton, and DAML SDK versions. Identify likely DAML-LF target compatibility issues before I build.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot Package IDs
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me diagnose a Canton package ID mismatch. Walk through LF target, SDK, stdlib, and dependency checks.

prompt_templates.title: Assess an LF Target Change
prompt_templates.prompt: Review a planned move from DAML-LF 2.1 to 2.2 for Splice packages. List required file changes, build risks, and validation checks.

output_examples.input: Which files control DAML-LF target versions in Splice?
output_examples.output: A concise map of the Splice configuration files, the package target files, and the build settings that affect DAML-LF output.

output_examples.input: Why did my package ID change after rebuilding?
output_examples.output: An explanation that package IDs depend on source content, LF target, SDK libraries, and dependencies, plus a checklist for comparing builds.

output_examples.input: Can I build community Canton with LF 2.2 packages?
output_examples.output: A cautious compatibility summary with the documented repository areas to inspect and the local build commands to review before execution.

best_practices: Confirm upstream release versions before using embedded version examples.
Ask before running build commands or repository-wide file replacements.
Keep LF target, SDK version, and dependency versions consistent across packages.
anti_patterns: Do not assume package IDs remain stable after changing LF target versions.
Do not mix enterprise and community DARs without checking compatibility.
Do not run documented shell commands in a repository without reviewing their effects.

faq.question: What does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers repository relationships, version mapping, build context, and troubleshooting for DAML SDK, Canton, and Splice.

faq.question: Does it fetch the latest Canton Network releases?
faq.answer: No. It uses embedded guidance and should be checked against official release pages for current versions.

faq.question: Can it run builds for me?
faq.answer: It can explain documented build commands, but command execution should require explicit user approval.

faq.question: Why are DAML-LF targets important?
faq.answer: LF targets affect package IDs, compatibility, and upgrade validation across Canton deployments.

faq.question: Is this skill useful in Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides repository and build guidance that can support code review and troubleshooting in those tools.

faq.question: What should I verify before applying its guidance?
faq.answer: Verify tool versions, release notes, local repository state, and the impact of any file edits or build commands.
